subject: FOIA Request - Public Works Overtime and Emergency Snow Contract Records
body_clean:
To the Village Clerk and Mayor Okonkwo,

Pursuant to the Illinois Freedom of Information Act, 5 ILCS 140, I am submitting the following request for public records. Please treat this email as a formal FOIA request as of today's date.

I request copies of the following, for the period July 1, 2024 through January 31, 2025:

1. All Public Works payroll records reflecting overtime hours and overtime pay, broken out by employee classification (not by name if you assert an exemption), including the date worked, hours, and rate paid.

2. All time sheets, call-out logs, and supervisor authorizations for overtime worked during the snow and ice events of January 5-6, 2025 and January 17-19, 2025.

3. The full contract, purchase order, and any change orders for the emergency snow-hauling and salt-replenishment work performed in January 2025, including the name of the vendor, the not-to-exceed amount, and the legal basis under which competitive bidding was waived.

4. Any memo, email, or determination by the Village Manager or Mayor declaring an emergency that justified a no-bid award under the Village's purchasing ordinance.

5. The current Public Works overtime budget line for FY2025 (account number and adopted amount) and a year-to-date expenditure report against that line.

I have reason to believe the Village has substantially exceeded its budgeted overtime authority this winter and entered into a no-bid contract exceeding $25,000 without the documentation the ordinance requires. The public is entitled to see these records. I am a resident and an officer of the Bellwood Taxpayers Alliance; this request is not for commercial purposes, so no commercial-rate fee applies. Please advise of any copying fees in advance if they exceed $20.

I expect a response within the five business days the statute allows.

subject: Acknowledgment of FOIA Request - PW Overtime and Snow Contract
body_clean:
Dear Mr. Carter,

This acknowledges receipt of your Freedom of Information Act request received in this office at 9:14 a.m. today. The request has been logged as FOIA No. 2025-041. Under 5 ILCS 140/3(d), the Village has five business days from the date of receipt to respond. That deadline falls on the close of business February 14, 2025.

Your request is broad and touches multiple departments (Public Works payroll, Finance budget reporting, and the Village Manager's emergency procurement file). Some responsive records, particularly payroll detail and the call-out logs, will require collection and review for material that is exempt under 5 ILCS 140/7(1)(b) (private personal information such as home addresses, personal phone numbers, and the last four of Social Security numbers that appear on time sheets) and 7(1)(c) (unwarranted invasion of personal privacy). Those exemptions will be applied by redaction only; the underlying overtime hours, dates, classifications, and dollar amounts you are seeking are not exempt and will be produced.

Please be advised that under 5 ILCS 140/3(e), I may extend the response period by up to five additional business days if the request requires collection of records from separate departments or examination of a voluminous set of records, both of which apply here. If I invoke an extension I will notify you in writing before February 14 with the statutory basis.

There is no charge for the first 50 pages of black-and-white letter-size copies. Beyond that, the fee is $0.15 per page. I anticipate providing the bulk of this electronically as PDF at no cost. I will route the budget and procurement portions to Finance Director Okafor and Village Manager Whitfield today.

subject: Overtime Figures and Budget Lines for FOIA 2025-041
body_clean:
Yolanda, Mayor, and Frank,

Here is the budget data responsive to items 1 and 5 of the Carter FOIA, compiled from the general ledger through January 31. I want everyone working from the same numbers before anything goes out the door.

The Public Works overtime line is account 01-40-5130 (Salaries - Overtime, Streets & Snow Division). The FY2025 adopted budget for that line is $148,000. Year-to-date actual expenditure through January 31 is $211,640, which is 143% of the full-year appropriation with five months of the fiscal year still to run. The overage is concentrated almost entirely in January: $96,310 of overtime was charged in that single month against a monthly run-rate that had been averaging about $14,000 July through December.

For context, the snow events drove the spike. The January 5-6 lake-effect event generated 312 overtime hours; the January 17-19 ice-and-thaw event generated 488 overtime hours, several crews running 16-hour shifts. At time-and-a-half on an average loaded rate of roughly $58/hour, that is consistent with the dollars posted.

Separately, the emergency snow-hauling and salt purchase (item 3) was charged to account 01-40-5440 (Contractual - Snow Operations), not to overtime, in the amount of $41,250. I flag that because Mr. Carter's request conflates the two; we should make clear in the response that overtime and the contract are distinct line items so he cannot claim we are hiding the contract inside payroll.

None of these figures are exempt. They are exactly the kind of expenditure data FOIA is designed to disclose, and the budget is a public document already. I recommend we produce the YTD expenditure report and the adopted-budget page in full. I will need a board appropriation transfer or a supplemental to cover the 5130 overage before April; the Mayor should be aware that question is coming regardless of the FOIA.

subject: Justification for January Snow Overtime and Emergency Hauling
body_clean:
Mayor, David, Karen, Yolanda,

Before this gets framed as Public Works "overspending," I want the operational record in front of all of you, because I will stand behind every hour my crews worked.

The January 17-19 event was not an ordinary snowfall. We had 7.2 inches of wet snow followed by a 9-degree overnight drop that flash-froze it to pavement across the entire grid, then a partial thaw that put standing water and refreeze on St. Charles Rd, Washington Blvd, Mannheim, 25th Ave, and every Metra access route around the UP-W station. I had 11 plows and salt spreaders running continuously. State law and our own snow-and-ice policy require us to keep arterials and emergency routes passable; I was not going to leave the hospital route or the school routes glazed over to protect a budget line.

We burned through 940 tons of salt in 72 hours and our dome was effectively empty. Our normal salt vendor under the Cook County joint purchasing contract could not deliver until the following week. That is why I called Verdi Excavating for emergency snow-hauling of the windrows blocking the St. Charles Rd business frontage and for an emergency salt transfer. I documented the justification the same day and Karen signed the emergency determination. The $41,250 is hauling, equipment, and material at the emergency rate; it is high, but the alternative was impassable arterials and a real liability exposure if someone went off the road.

On overtime: yes, $96,000 in one month looks alarming on paper. But the contractor alternative for the plowing itself would have cost far more, and these are our people who know the routes. I have the call-out logs, the shift sheets, and the salt-usage reports ready for Yolanda to redact and release. I have nothing to hide. I would just ask that whatever goes out includes the weather and tonnage context so this does not read like we handed out money.

subject: Notice of Five-Business-Day Extension - FOIA 2025-041
body_clean:
Dear Mr. Carter,

This is written notice under 5 ILCS 140/3(e) that the Village is extending the time to respond to FOIA No. 2025-041 by five additional business days. The new response date is the close of business February 21, 2025.

The statutory grounds for the extension, which I am required to state, are 3(e)(iii), that the request requires the collection of a substantial number of specified records, and 3(e)(v), that the request requires records be collected from establishments or facilities other than the office processing the request. Your request reaches payroll detail held by Public Works, expenditure and budget reports held by Finance, and the emergency procurement determination held by the Village Manager. The call-out logs and time sheets for the January 5-6 and January 17-19 events alone run to several hundred pages, each of which must be reviewed line by line to redact private personal information under 7(1)(b).

I want to be transparent with you about what you will and will not receive. You will receive: the FY2025 adopted budget page for account 01-40-5130, the year-to-date expenditure report through January 31, the full Verdi Excavating contract and purchase order with the not-to-exceed amount, the Village Manager's emergency determination memo, and the overtime hours, dates, classifications, and dollar amounts. You will not receive, in unredacted form: employee home addresses, personal cell numbers, the last four digits of Social Security numbers, and direct-deposit banking information, all of which appear on the raw payroll printouts and all of which are exempt under 7(1)(b). The overtime figures themselves are not being withheld.

If any portion is denied or redacted in the final response, that letter will cite the specific exemption for each redaction and will advise you of your right to review by the Public Access Counselor and your right to judicial review, as 5 ILCS 140/9 requires.

subject: Objection to Extension and Demand for Emergency Procurement Basis
body_clean:
Ms. Pierce,

I received your extension notice and I am registering my objection on the record, though I understand the statute permits one extension of up to five business days. What I will not accept is the extension being used to narrow what gets produced.

Two points.

First, on the redactions: I never asked for anyone's Social Security number or bank account. Redact those, fine. But I am specifically requesting the overtime broken out so the public can see the magnitude, and I want the supervisor authorization signatures on the call-out logs left intact. Who approved 16-hour shifts and 488 hours in a weekend is a matter of public accountability, not personal privacy. The names of public employees acting in their official capacity, and the names of the supervisors who authorized public expenditures, are not exempt under 7(1)(b), and the Public Access Counselor has issued binding and advisory opinions saying exactly that. Do not redact the authorizing official's name.

Second, and more important, on the no-bid contract: a $41,250 emergency award to Verdi Excavating without competitive bidding is precisely the kind of thing that requires a written, contemporaneous emergency determination. Your office's own purchasing ordinance, as I read it, caps non-bid purchases at $25,000 and requires board ratification of emergency awards above that at the next regular meeting. I want to see (a) the date the determination memo was actually signed, not back-dated, and (b) the minutes showing the board ratified or was even told about this award. If the board was never asked to ratify a $41,250 sole-source contract, that is a violation independent of FOIA, and I will say so publicly.

I will hold until February 21. If the response is incomplete, redacts authorizing officials, or fails to produce the emergency determination and ratification record, I intend to file a Request for Review with the Public Access Counselor at the Office of the Illinois Attorney General. Please do not make that necessary.

subject: Direction to Staff on FOIA 2025-041 - Full Transparency
body_clean:
Karen, David, Frank, Yolanda,

I have read the full chain, including Mr. Carter's latest. I want to set the direction for this office clearly and in writing.

We are going to produce these records fully and on time. I am not interested in fighting a FOIA over expenditure data that is, frankly, defensible on the merits. Frank's crews kept the arterials open during a genuinely dangerous event; David's numbers explain the spike; and an open, complete response is the strongest possible answer to an allegation of hiding something. The worst thing we could do politically and legally is look like we are stonewalling.

Specific instructions:

Yolanda, on the redactions, Mr. Carter is right on the law and I want us on the right side of it. Redact Social Security digits, home addresses, personal cell numbers, and banking information. Do not redact the names or signatures of supervisors and managers who authorized overtime or signed the emergency determination. Those are public employees acting officially, and the PAC has been consistent that authorization of public expenditure is disclosable. Releasing it voluntarily is better than being ordered to.

Karen, I need you to pull the actual signed-and-dated emergency determination memo for the Verdi award and confirm the date it was executed. If it was signed contemporaneously in January, we produce it and we are fine. If there is any gap, I need to know today, not after it goes out the door.

David, I want the Verdi award on the agenda for the next regular board meeting for ratification if it has not already been ratified. If our ordinance required board action above $25,000 and we missed it, we cure it now, on the record, rather than letting Mr. Carter discover it. I would rather take the criticism for a process miss we fixed than for one we buried.

We respond complete by February 21. No extensions beyond this one.

subject: Emergency Determination Date and Ratification Status - Verdi Award
body_clean:
Mayor, David, Yolanda,

Responding to the Mayor's direction. I pulled the Verdi Excavating emergency procurement file this afternoon.

The good news: the emergency determination memo is real and contemporaneous. It is dated January 18, 2025, signed by me at 6:40 a.m. during the second storm, and it recites the specific findings, that arterial routes including St. Charles Rd, Washington Blvd, and the Metra access roads were impassable, that the salt dome was depleted to roughly 60 tons, and that the Cook County joint-purchasing salt vendor could not deliver before January 27. It cites our purchasing ordinance Section 2-241(c), the emergency exception, and it is time-stamped in our document system, so there is no back-dating question. Frank's account matches the memo. We produce it in full; it helps us.

The less good news, and I want to be straight with you because the Mayor asked: under Section 2-241(d), an emergency award exceeding $25,000 is supposed to be brought to the Board for ratification at the next regular meeting. The next regular meeting was February 3. The Verdi award was not on that agenda. It was an oversight on my part, the snow recovery and the salt restocking consumed the staff and it fell through the cracks. So Mr. Carter's procedural point has some merit, and I am not going to pretend otherwise.

My recommendation: we place the Verdi ratification on the February 18 special meeting agenda or, at the latest, the March 3 regular meeting, with a transmittal memo explaining the emergency, the determination, the amount, and the reason ratification was delayed. We produce that transmittal and the eventual minutes to Mr. Carter as a supplement once they exist. I would rather hand him the cure than have him find the gap. I take responsibility for the missed ratification and I will say so to the Board.

subject: Final Response and Production - FOIA 2025-041
body_clean:
Dear Mr. Carter,

This is the Village's final response to FOIA No. 2025-041, provided within the extended deadline of February 21, 2025. Responsive records are attached as a single indexed PDF of 287 pages. The production is itemized to your numbered requests.

Item 1 (overtime by classification): Produced in full. A summary schedule shows FY2025 overtime in account 01-40-5130 of $211,640 year-to-date against an adopted budget of $148,000, with the January detail broken out by classification (Maintenance Worker I/II, Equipment Operator, Streets Foreman) and by date.

Item 2 (time sheets, call-out logs, supervisor authorizations for January 5-6 and January 17-19): Produced. Redactions are limited to employee home addresses, personal telephone numbers, the last four digits of Social Security numbers, and direct-deposit information, each withheld under 5 ILCS 140/7(1)(b). Consistent with the Mayor's direction and with PAC precedent, the names and signatures of the authorizing supervisors and the Streets Foreman who approved the overtime have NOT been redacted. The records show the 312 hours for the first event and the 488 hours for the second.

Item 3 (Verdi Excavating contract): Produced in full, including the purchase order, the not-to-exceed amount of $41,250, and the scope (emergency snow-hauling and emergency salt transfer).

Item 4 (emergency determination): Produced in full. The determination memo signed January 18, 2025 by Village Manager Whitfield, citing purchasing ordinance Section 2-241(c), is included with its system time-stamp.

Item 5 (budget line and YTD report): Produced in full.

In the interest of completeness I am disclosing, although it is not yet a record, that the Board ratification of the Verdi emergency award required under Section 2-241(d) was not placed on the February 3 agenda and has been scheduled for the March 3 regular meeting. The transmittal memo and resulting minutes will be supplemented to you when they exist.

No portion of your request is being denied. Because there is no denial, no exemption-by-exemption denial letter is required, but you are nonetheless advised of your right under 5 ILCS 140/9.5 to file a Request for Review with the Public Access Counselor, and your right to judicial review.

subject: Response Received - Reserving PAC Appeal on Ratification Failure
body_clean:
Mayor Okonkwo, Ms. Pierce, Ms. Whitfield,

I received the 287-page production this morning and I have reviewed it. I will give credit where it is due. The FOIA response itself is, on its face, complete and timely. You produced the overtime detail, you left the authorizing supervisors' names unredacted as the law requires, and you produced the Verdi contract and the January 18 emergency determination without games. That is more transparency than I expected, and I acknowledge it publicly as well as privately. On the FOIA compliance question, I have no grounds for a Public Access Counselor appeal, and I will not file one. The records request is satisfied.

That said, the records you produced confirm the substance of my original concern, and on that I am not standing down. You spent $211,640 against a $148,000 overtime appropriation, a 43% overrun, and you awarded a $41,250 sole-source emergency contract that, by Ms. Whitfield's own admission, was never brought to the Board for the ratification your ordinance Section 2-241(d) requires at the next regular meeting. The emergency may well have been genuine. The plowing may well have been necessary. But the ordinance is not optional, and "it fell through the cracks" is not ratification.

So here is where I land. I will attend the March 3 meeting. If the Board ratifies the Verdi award with a full public accounting, and if the Mayor brings forward the appropriation transfer to cover the 5130 overrun on the record, I will consider the matter properly cured and I will say so. If the ratification is quietly tabled, buried in a consent agenda, or skipped again, then my appeal will not be a FOIA appeal, it will be a formal complaint to the Board and a referral to the County regarding the procurement, and I will reserve every right to escalate to the Attorney General's office on the underlying spending. Transparency on the records was the right call. Now finish the job on the substance.

I appreciate that this office did not make me fight for the documents. Few do.
